pattern: A removal only ever cuts a single contiguous piece out of the array, so what is left is always some strictly increasing prefix glued to some strictly increasing suffix -- precomputing which prefixes and suffixes qualify turns every removal check into a constant-time lookup.
hints: Whatever subarray you remove, the leftover pieces are always a prefix of the array and a suffix of the array stuck together -- so first figure out which prefixes, and which suffixes, are themselves strictly increasing on their own.

For every possible kept-prefix length and kept-suffix start, the only extra thing to check is whether the last kept prefix value is smaller than the first kept suffix value (or whether one side is empty).

Precompute a boolean for every prefix length 'is this prefix strictly increasing' and every suffix start 'is this suffix strictly increasing', then count every pair of a valid prefix length i and suffix start j with i < j where the boundary condition holds.

edge_inputs.description: single element, its own (empty) removal-adjacent array always counts
already strictly increasing, so every contiguous piece is removable
strictly decreasing, only removals leaving one side empty or a single survivor work
a flat run in the middle blocks some removals that look plausible
duplicate value straddling where the prefix and suffix would meet
two equal endpoints with an increasing middle, testing the boundary comparison
all values identical, only full-array removal (leaving nothing) or single survivors count

stress.description: random small-alphabet arrays at the maximum allowed length to exercise many prefix/suffix combinations
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(50, size))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 50) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
